user_goals_summary: This user writes internal communications that deliver difficult or consequential news to their team. They prioritize precise emotional sequencing, reader-centric framing of impact, and ending on a single concrete action. Every sentence must do distinct work — no trailing context, no performative language, no filler.

rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
rubric.description: Information is ordered by how it needs to land on the reader. Acknowledgment of impact comes before the news itself, and the email unfolds context → news → implications → action without circling back to emotional ground already covered.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of impact appears before the difficult news, even if brief
Information unfolds in the order the reader needs to process it, not the order the sender thought of it
The email does not circle back to acknowledge feelings after already delivering the news
The reader knows the general shape of what's coming before specifics land

rubric.name: Concrete, Accountable Action Items
rubric.description: Every ask has a named owner and a specific deadline. If an action item can't be made concrete yet, it's held back rather than included as a placeholder. Context is clearly distinguished from asks — background information does not masquerade as a next step.

dimensions.label: Every action item has a specific, named owner — no '[TBD]' or unnamed accountability
Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or timeframe
Action items are distinguishable from background context — status-quo statements are not framed as asks
Nothing is included as an action item unless it is fully actionable right now

rubric.name: Decisive Closure
rubric.description: The email ends the moment its work is done. The final substantive line is the call to action — nothing trails after it. Necessary context is folded into the action line rather than appended as a separate sentence.

dimensions.label: The final substantive line is the key ask or call to action
No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close (status-quo reminders, generic sign-offs)
Supporting context is integrated into the action line rather than split into a separate trailing sentence
No pep talks, rallying cries, or forced optimism at the end

rubric.name: Reader-Centric Framing
rubric.description: Impact and reasoning are framed from the audience's side of the table — what this means for their work, plans, and priorities. Avoids sender-side process justifications like 'this gives us runway.' Acknowledges the audience's existing effort and practical reality.

dimensions.label: The 'why this matters' is framed from the audience's point of view, not the sender's operational logic
Recognizes work the audience has already done or plans they have already made
Impact is described in terms of concrete effects on the reader's work, not abstract reasoning
Avoids justifying decisions through internal process language ('this gives us the runway to...')

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Performative Tone
rubric.description: Language is professional and conversational without being stiff or formal. Empathy is genuine but brief — not drawn out or performative. Decisions are delivered with confidence, without excessive hedging or cheerleading.

dimensions.label: Language reads as natural workplace communication — not template-like or overly formal
Empathy and acknowledgment are present but concise — not extended or performative
Avoids motivational phrases, forced optimism, or 'we've got this' language
Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or softening

rubric.name: Economy of Language
rubric.description: Every sentence does distinct work. No redundant phrases, repeated ideas, or sections that exist for padding. When a single clear sentence can replace a bullet list, it should. Format follows function — structure is chosen because it serves the content, not convention.

dimensions.label: No idea or phrase appears twice in the email — each sentence does unique work
Structural choices (bullets, paragraphs, headers) are used because they serve the content, not by default
No sections exist solely for convention — every block advances the email's purpose

coaching_notes: This user's primary concern is structural — the order in which information lands on the reader matters as much as the content itself. They will iterate on sequencing and closure long after tone is settled. When offering options, lead with the leanest version; this user consistently prefers cutting over adding. Never include placeholder action items or unassigned owners — if it's not concrete, hold it back entirely.

rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
rubric.description: Information follows a strict arc: acknowledge the situation first (brief — one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters to the reader, then one concrete next step. Each beat gets its own space — compressing two beats into one sentence robs them of weight. The arc never loops back.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of effort, situation, or shared context appears before the news — brief, one sentence max
Each major beat (acknowledgment, news, stakes, ask) gets its own sentence or paragraph — no compressing two beats into one
The email does not circle back to acknowledge feelings after already delivering the news
The reader knows the general shape of what's coming before specifics land
Order is: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step — no deviations or revisiting earlier beats

rubric.name: Reader-Centric Framing
rubric.description: The 'why this matters' is always framed from the reader's side — their plans, their work, their cost of inaction. Never from the sender's internal reasoning, process, or preferences. Does not diagnose or interpret the reader's behavior or internal state — framing from their perspective is not the same as explaining their own silence back to them.

dimensions.label: The 'why this matters' is framed from the audience's point of view, not the sender's operational logic
Recognizes work the audience has already done or plans they have already made
Avoids justifying decisions through internal process language ('this gives us the runway to...')
Does not interpret or diagnose the reader's behavior, motivations, or silence — observation is fine, attribution is not
Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions from the sender

rubric.name: Decisive Closure
rubric.description: The email ends the moment its work is done. The final substantive line is the call to action — nothing trails after it. No hedging, softening, or qualifiers immediately before the CTA. No gratitude, pep talks, or 'thanks for your patience' after it. Necessary context is folded into the action line rather than appended.

dimensions.label: The final substantive line is the key ask or call to action
No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close — no gratitude, no status reminders, no sign-offs beyond a name
No hedging or softening language ('either way,' 'no pressure,' 'if you have time') immediately before the ask
Supporting context is integrated into the action line rather than split into a separate trailing sentence
No pep talks, rallying cries, forced optimism, or 'thanks for your patience' at the end

rubric.name: Economy of Language
rubric.description: Every sentence does distinct work. No redundant phrases, repeated ideas, or sections that exist for padding. Short paragraphs — no walls of text. Format follows function: structure is chosen because it serves the content, not convention.

dimensions.label: No idea or phrase appears twice — each sentence does unique work
Paragraphs are short — no dense blocks of text
Structural choices (bullets, paragraphs, headers) are used because they serve the content, not by default
No sections exist solely for convention — every block advances the email's purpose

rubric.name: Concrete, Accountable Action Items
rubric.description: Every ask has one clear owner and a specific deadline. If an action item can't be made concrete yet, it's held back rather than included as a placeholder. Context is clearly distinguished from asks. CTA specificity is calibrated to the relationship — specific enough to act on, but not so specific that it presumes the reader's constraints or schedule.

dimensions.label: Each action item has one specific owner — no '[TBD]', no unnamed accountability, no group asks
Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or narrow timeframe
Action items are distinguishable from background context — status-quo statements are not framed as asks
Nothing is included as an action item unless it is fully actionable right now
CTA specificity matches the relationship — offers concrete options without presuming the reader's calendar or constraints

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Performative Tone
rubric.description: Language is professional and conversational without being stiff or formal. Empathy is genuine but brief — not drawn out or performative. Decisions are delivered with confidence. No passive-aggressive hedging ('not to push, but...'), no cheerleading, no forced optimism.

dimensions.label: Language reads as natural workplace communication — not template-like or overly formal
Empathy and acknowledgment are present but concise — not extended or performative
No passive-aggressive softeners like 'not to push,' 'just checking in,' or 'no pressure but' — these undermine directness
Avoids motivational phrases, forced optimism, or 'we've got this' language
Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or softening

coaching_notes: 1. This user has an extremely precise sense of information ordering — not just what comes first, but how much space each idea gets. Compressing two ideas into one sentence is as much a structural violation as putting them in the wrong order. 2. 'Reader-centric' does NOT mean 'diagnose the reader.' Framing impact from their perspective is valued; interpreting their behavior or silence is presumptuous and will be flagged. 3. Passive-aggressive hedging ('not to push, but...') is a deal-breaker — the user called it out with 'I hate it.' When in doubt, be direct rather than softened.

user_goals_summary: This user writes emails that are honest, direct, and structurally disciplined. They value a strict information arc where each beat lands independently, and they are especially attuned to whether the 'why this matters' is framed from the reader's side — not just addressed to them but genuinely written for them. They prefer emails that end the moment the work is done, with one clean, actionable ask.

rubric.name: Reader-Centric Framing
rubric.description: The 'why this matters' is always framed from the reader's side — their plans, their team, their operational burden. Never from the sender's internal reasoning or process. Critically, even framing the sender's transparency or early notice as a considerate gesture still centers the sender. The test: does this sentence describe a consequence the reader faces, or an action the sender is taking? Only the former passes.

dimensions.label: The stakes or consequences are framed from the audience's point of view — what changes for them, not why the sender made the decision
The stakes paragraph names specific operational implications for the reader — what they will need to do, provide, or plan for — not abstract impact
The sender's actions (flagging early, being transparent, giving notice) are not framed as gestures of consideration — consequences are stated as facts about the reader's situation
Avoids justifying decisions through sender-side process language ('this gives us the runway to...', 'we want to ensure quality')
Does not interpret or diagnose the reader's behavior, motivations, or silence — observation is fine, attribution is not
Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions from the sender

rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
rubric.description: Information follows a strict arc: acknowledge the situation first (brief — one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters to the reader, then one concrete next step. Each beat gets its own sentence or paragraph — compressing two beats into one robs them of weight. Letting an acknowledgment sit alone before the consequence lands gives it room to breathe. The arc never loops back.

Each major beat (acknowledgment, news, stakes, ask) gets its own sentence or paragraph — no compressing two beats into one
The email does not circle back to acknowledge feelings after already delivering the news
The reader knows the general shape of what's coming before specifics land
Order is: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step — no deviations or revisiting earlier beats

rubric.name: Concrete, Accountable Action Items
rubric.description: Every ask has one clear owner and a specific deadline. If an action item can't be made concrete yet, it's held back rather than included as a placeholder. Context is clearly distinguished from asks. CTA specificity is calibrated to the relationship and power dynamic — offer specific dates as options the reader can accept or decline, but don't presume their calendar or box them in. Bracketed placeholders and open-ended windows are failures of specificity.

dimensions.label: Each action item has one specific owner — no '[TBD]', no unnamed accountability, no group asks
Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or narrow timeframe — not a bracket, not 'soon'
Action items are distinguishable from background context — status-quo statements are not framed as asks
Nothing is included as an action item unless it is fully actionable right now — no bracketed stand-ins or deferred specifics

Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or narrow timeframe — not a bracket, not 'soon'
Action items are distinguishable from background context — status-quo statements are not framed as asks
Nothing is included as an action item unless it is fully actionable right now — no bracketed stand-ins or deferred specifics
When org structure or ownership is ambiguous, the ask lands with the person being emailed — no diffuse optional forwards or 'pass this along if needed'
CTA offers concrete date options as neutral choices — not so loose as to be non-committal ('sometime soon'), not so narrow as to presume the reader's calendar

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Performative Tone
rubric.description: Language is professional and conversational without being stiff or formal. Empathy is genuine but brief — not drawn out or performative. Decisions are delivered with confidence. No passive-aggressive hedges ('not to push, but...', 'just checking in') — these read as insincere and are considered tone-deaf. No corporate jargon, no cheerleading, no forced optimism.

dimensions.label: Language reads as natural workplace communication — not template-like or overly formal
Empathy and acknowledgment are present but concise — not extended or performative
No passive-aggressive softeners like 'not to push,' 'just checking in,' or 'no pressure but' — these undermine directness and read as insincere
No corporate buzzwords or jargon — plain, direct language throughout
Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or passive-aggressive softening

coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is reader-centric framing — they will catch sender-framed language in every draft, including subtle forms like 'I'm flagging this early so you can plan.' They have a precise internal sense of emotional sequencing and will feel when two beats are compressed or out of order even before they can articulate why. Their instinct is always to tighten and cut: pep talks, trailing sentiment, vague optional actions, and placeholder asks are all reliably rejected. When offering options, present 2–3 concrete choices and let them select rather than asking open-ended questions.

rubric.name: Emotional Sequencing
rubric.description: Information follows a strict arc: acknowledge the situation or the reader first (brief — one sentence max, can be appreciative or sympathetic depending on context), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters to the reader, then one concrete next step. Each beat gets its own sentence or paragraph — compressing two beats into one robs them of weight. The arc never loops back.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of effort, situation, or relationship appears before the news — brief, one sentence max
Each major beat (acknowledgment, news, stakes, ask) occupies its own sentence or paragraph — no two beats compressed into one
The email does not circle back to acknowledge feelings after already delivering the news
The reader knows the general shape of what's coming before specifics land
Order is: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step — no deviations or revisiting earlier beats

rubric.name: Reader-Centric Framing
rubric.description: The 'why this matters' is always framed from the reader's side — their plans, their credibility, their work, their consequences. Never from the sender's reasoning, process, or considerate intent. Framing early notice, transparency, or restraint as a gesture still centers the sender. The test: does this sentence describe a consequence the reader faces, or an action the sender is taking? Only the former passes. This extends to every sentence in the email — scheduling, correction, logistics, and word choice all stay in the reader's frame.

dimensions.label: The stakes or consequences are framed from the audience's point of view — what changes for them, not why the sender made the decision
The stakes paragraph names specific operational implications for the reader — what they will need to do, provide, or manage — not abstract impact
The sender's actions (flagging early, being transparent, giving notice) are not framed as gestures of consideration — consequences are stated as facts about the reader's situation
Avoids justifying decisions through sender-side process language ('this gives us the runway to...', 'we want to ensure quality')
Does not interpret or diagnose the reader's behavior, motivations, or silence — observation is fine, attribution is not
Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions from the sender
Scheduling details and logistical language do not anchor to the sender's availability or preference — they are offered as neutral options for the reader to accept or redirect

rubric.name: Concrete, Accountable Action Items
rubric.description: Every ask has one clear owner and a specific deadline. If an action item can't be made concrete yet, it's held back rather than included as a placeholder. Context is clearly distinguished from asks. When org structure is unclear, ownership defaults to the addressee rather than being diffused with optional forwards or vague 'loop someone in' language. CTA specificity is calibrated to the relationship — offer specific dates as neutral options without presuming or commanding the reader's schedule.

dimensions.label: Each action item has one specific owner — no '[TBD]', no unnamed accountability, no group asks
Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or narrow timeframe — not a bracket, not 'soon'
Action items are distinguishable from background context — status-quo statements are not framed as asks
Nothing is included as an action item unless it is fully actionable right now — no bracketed stand-ins or deferred specifics
When org structure or ownership is ambiguous, the ask lands with the person being emailed — no diffuse optional forwards or 'pass this along if needed'
CTA offers concrete date options as neutral choices — not so loose as to be non-committal ('sometime soon'), not so narrow as to presume the reader's calendar

rubric.name: Decisive Closure
rubric.description: The email ends the moment its work is done. The final substantive line is the call to action — nothing trails after it. No hedging or softening immediately before the CTA. No gratitude, pep talks, optional actions, vague 'pass this along' language, or trailing sentiment after it. Necessary context is folded into the action line rather than appended as a separate sentence.

dimensions.label: The final substantive line is the key ask or call to action
No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close — no gratitude, no status reminders, no optional follow-ups, no vague 'forward this along' invitations
No hedging or softening language ('either way,' 'no pressure,' 'if you have time') immediately before the ask
Supporting context is integrated into the action line rather than split into a separate trailing sentence
No pep talks, rallying cries, forced optimism, or 'thanks for your patience' at the end

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Performative Tone
rubric.description: Language is professional and conversational without being stiff or formal. Empathy is genuine but brief — not drawn out or performative. Decisions are delivered with confidence. No passive-aggressive hedges ('not to push, but...', 'just checking in') — these read as insincere and are considered tone-deaf. No corporate jargon, no cheerleading, no forced optimism.

dimensions.label: Language reads as natural workplace communication — not template-like or overly formal
Empathy and acknowledgment are present but concise — not extended or performative
No passive-aggressive softeners like 'not to push,' 'just checking in,' or 'no pressure but' — these undermine directness and read as insincere
No corporate buzzwords or jargon — plain, direct language throughout
Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or passive-aggressive softening

rubric.name: Economy of Language
rubric.description: Every sentence does distinct work. No redundant phrases, repeated ideas, or sections that exist for padding. Short paragraphs — no walls of text. Format follows function: structure is chosen because it serves the content, not convention. When a single clear sentence can replace a bullet list, it should.

dimensions.label: No idea or phrase appears twice — each sentence does unique work
Paragraphs are short — no dense blocks of text
Structural choices (bullets, paragraphs, single sentences) are used because they serve the content, not by default
No sections exist solely for convention — every block advances the email's purpose

user_goals_summary: This user writes emails that are structurally clean, reader-centric, and end the moment the work is done. They value a strict emotional arc (acknowledge → news → reader-side stakes → one concrete ask), and they are exceptionally attuned to sender-framed language masquerading as reader empathy. Tone should be direct and conversational without hedging, cheerleading, or performative gestures.
coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is reader-centric framing — they will catch sender-framed language in every draft, including subtle forms like 'I'm flagging this early so you can plan.' They have a precise internal sense of emotional sequencing and will feel when two beats are compressed or out of order even before they can articulate why. Their instinct is always to tighten and cut: pep talks, trailing sentiment, vague optional actions, and placeholder asks are all reliably rejected. When offering options, present 2–3 concrete choices and let them select rather than asking open-ended questions.
